exemplar.title: Investigating  the Effect of Anchoring Bias on Numerical Estimation

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating  the Effect of Anchoring Bias on Numerical Estimation

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

files.fileName: Investigating  the Effect of Anchoring Bias on Numerical Estimation.pdf

strengths: Aim is clearly stated and relevance is explicitly justified linking everyday calculations, educational assessment and System 1 thinking.
Dual Process Model is accurately described and explicitly connected to the anchoring task.
Independent and dependent variables are clearly stated and fully operationalised.
weaknesses: Minor formatting errors in anchor sequence notation and truncated math notation.
Aim could specify sequence length and timing parameters more precisely.
Hypothesis could include expected effect size or magnitude for greater precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three introduction strands meet the 5â6 band descriptors: the aim is clearly stated and its relevance well explained; the theory is correctly described with explicit linkage to the study; and variables are operationalised. Minor formatting lapses and missing details prevent a perfect 6, so a score of 5 reflects very strong performance with room for minor improvements.

strengths: The independent-samples research design is explained with its strengths and a noted limitation.
Opportunity sampling and participant characteristics are described and linked to demand characteristics and generalisability.
Controlled variables and materials are listed and their purposes briefly explained.
weaknesses: Explanation of random assignment could be clearer to address potential confounds.
Discussion of sampling bias and homogeneity is not deeply analytical.
Rationale for specific controls (e.g., video duration) could be expanded.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each exploration strand achieves the top band descriptor for being explained rather than merely described, but the depth is moderate rather than comprehensive. The student clearly explains design, sampling and controls yet does not demonstrate the thorough critical analysis needed for full marks, justifying a bandâhigh score of 3 out of 4.

strengths: Descriptive (mean, SD, range) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics are appropriately chosen, justified and correctly interpreted.
Graphical presentation is accurate, clearly labelled, includes error bars, and directly addresses the hypothesis.
Findings interpretation links statistical results to the data and to the research hypothesis, indicating rejection of the null.
weaknesses: Typographical errors (e.g., 'Whintey') and omission of sample sizes in tables.
Findings interpretation has a truncated sentence and could report effect size or confidence intervals.
Graph could include confidence interval legends to strengthen interpretability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student consistently applies correct descriptive and inferential statistics, presents a clear graph, and interprets findings in relation to the hypothesis. Minor errors and missing supplementary details (effect size, n values) warrant a score of 5 out of 6 for analysis.

strengths: Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and fully justified in terms of validity and generalisability.
Strengths and limitations are clearly articulated and directly relevant to the hypothesis and procedure.
Discussion of findings is connected to the Dual Process Model and anchoring bias.
weaknesses: Findings discussion is relatively brief and lacks deeper critical engagement with alternative explanations.
Evaluation of sampling and procedure could further explore impacts on statistical power and fatigue effects.
Strengths/limitations discussion could integrate more nuanced trade-offs (e.g., extended time vs System 1 reliance).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation demonstrates clear discussion of findings with reference to theory, strengths and limitations that are explained and modifications that are fully justified, meeting the criteria for the 3â6 band. The brevity of the findings discussion and limited critical depth justify a midâband score of 4 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The bar graph with error bars clearly compares mean estimates, labels axes, includes numerical values, and directly addresses the hypothesis. No inaccurate scaling is evident. The presentation therefore satisfies âcorrectly presented and addresses the hypothesisâ, earning 5 marks in the top band.

The student states the statistical outcome (U, p, decision rule), **interprets it in relation to the data and links it back to rejecting the null and supporting the research hypothesis**. Additional comment on closeness to actual total further shows integration with data. This meets full-descriptor expectations; 5 marks awarded.

Both IV (type of numerical anchor: descending vs ascending) and DV (mean estimated sum) are **clearly stated and operationalised**. Operationalisation includes the precise sequence format and the measure (mean estimate) making it testable. This fits the 5-6 descriptor; wording is clear though concise, so a score of 5 is appropriate.

The student names opportunity sampling, details the school context and demographic profile, and **explains how these choices affect demand characteristics and generalisability**. This moves beyond mere description, fulfilling the top band for this criterion, but the rationale is not deeply analytical, so 3 marks are awarded.

The Dual Process Model is **accurately described** (System 1 vs System 2, heuristics, cognitive misers) and the link to anchoring within the planned investigation is **explicitly drawn**. The student shows how the bias emerges from System 1 reliance and why the present task should elicit it. This meets the requirement of describing the theory *and* explaining its relevance to the study, meriting a low-top-band score.

Findings are related to the Dual Process Model and anchoring bias, showing how System 1 reliance explains the observed effect. This is more than a surface mention but the discussion is relatively brief and lacks deeper critical engagement with alternative interpretations, so it fits the 3-4 band with 3 marks.

The student clearly states the aim (to examine how high vs. low numerical anchors influence estimates of an addition sequence) and **explicitly justifies relevance** by linking it to everyday calculations, educatorsâ assessment design, and understanding System 1 thinking. This surpasses a simple identification and moves beyond a token mention, fulfilling the descriptor for band 5-6. Minor phrasing lapses do not detract from a clear, well-reasoned explanation, warranting a score toward the lower end of the top band.

Multiple strengths (independent design, standardised video, reduced demand) and limitations (participant variability, small sample, socio-economic homogeneity) are **clearly explained and explicitly related to how they might influence the DV or validity**. Coverage is balanced and relevant, meeting the 5-6 descriptor; 5 marks granted.

Controlled variables (timing, question order) and materials (video stimuli, consent forms, answer sheets) are listed and **their purposes are briefly explained**âe.g., timing to induce System 1, video to standardise exposure. This meets the âexplainedâ requirement for band 3-4, though exposition is concise rather than thorough, justifying 3 marks.

Suggested changes (matching maths ability, diversifying sample, extending time) are **directly tied to named limitations and justified in terms of validity and generalisability**. Each proposal is clearly linked to the issue it addresses, fulfilling the criterion for fully justified modifications; 5 marks are appropriate.

The independent-samples design is not only described but **explained with strengths (no carry-over, reduced demand characteristics) and a limitation (between-class ability differences)**. The explanation is brief yet sufficient to satisfy the âexplainedâ criterion, fitting band 3-4. Depth is moderate rather than exhaustive, so 3 marks are allocated.

Appropriate descriptive (mean, SD, range) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics are applied. Calculations match raw data, the non-parametric choice is justified by small independent groups, and critical values/p-value are correctly interpreted. Minor typographical errors (âWhinteyâ) are cosmetic, so accurate application warrants 5 in the top band.

annotations.comment: The theory description accurately outlines System 1 and System 2 thinking and their link to heuristics, providing a solid foundation for the investigation.
Consider explicitly connecting the described heuristic processes to addition sequences rather than multiplication to clarify relevance of the Dual Process Model to this specific task.
The summary of Tversky and Kahnemanâs original study contains a formatting error in the high anchor sequence; correct the sequence notation to improve clarity.
Controlled variables are clearly identified; expand on how the preâanchor distraction sequences reduce participantsâ awareness of anchoring manipulation.
Ethical considerations are exhaustively covered; consider noting IB ethics review or supervisor approval to reinforce compliance.
The aim is wellâstated with relevance to everyday use of addition, but could be strengthened by specifying the exact sequence length and timing parameters upfront.
Justification effectively links the study to educational assessment and System 1 thinking, demonstrating solid relevance.
The IV description is clear, but the parenthetical math notation is truncated; ensure the full descending and ascending sequences are shown.
The DV is succinctly defined as the mean estimated sum, but consider specifying how many trials contribute to that mean.
The directional hypothesis is appropriately stated; consider adding an expected effect size or magnitude for greater precision.
Random splitting by preâexisting classes may introduce confounding ability differences; clarify the method of random assignment.
The strength/limitation discussion demonstrates insight, but further explain how betweenâclass variability specifically affects anchoring estimates.
Opportunity sampling is described and its bias noted; discuss how the homogenous IB cohort might affect generalizability of anchoring effects.
The sample section comprehensively details demographics and controls for math ability; link how selfâreported course level is used in data analysis.
Materials are listed in detail; deepen the explanation of why the video presentation duration and format were chosen as controls.
The multiâlevel procedure list is thorough but risks overwhelming the reader; consider simplifying or grouping related steps.
Table 1 presents mean, range, and SD effectively but omits sample size (n) for each condition; include N to contextualize variability.
The bar graph addresses the hypothesis clearly; adding confidence intervals or error bars with a legend would strengthen interpretability.
The findings interpretation initiates well but trails off midâsentence; ensure complete articulation of the trend before moving to statistical results.
Inferential statistics are correctly applied; consider reporting effect size (e.g., r) to convey practical significance of the anchoring effect.
The discussion links findings to the Dual Process Model but would be stronger by considering alternate cognitive explanations such as working memory load.
Research design evaluation is insightful; suggest matchedâpairs allocation to better control individual ability differences.
Sample evaluation notes bias but could explore how small N affects statistical power and confidence in generalizability.
Procedure evaluation is thorough; consider addressing potential learning or fatigue effects from practice trials impacting estimates.
Modifications are well justified and directly linked to limitations; include discussion of tradeâoffs, such as extended time affecting reliance on System 1.
The conclusion restates core findings but could be strengthened by proposing specific future research questions or contexts.
